frameworks.name: task-relevant autoencoder via classifier enhancement (TRACE)
frameworks.one_line: autoencoder jointly trained with a classifier to extract behaviorally-relevant latent representations

frameworks.name: standard autoencoder
frameworks.one_line: unsupervised reconstruction-only baseline for dimensionality reduction

frameworks.name: variational autoencoder
frameworks.one_line: probabilistic latent-variable baseline for dimensionality reduction

frameworks.name: principal component analysis
frameworks.one_line: linear variance-maximising baseline for dimensionality reduction

core_question: Can a task-relevant autoencoder recover behaviorally-meaningful low-dimensional neural representations from small, high-dimensional human neuroimaging datasets?
key_assumptions: behaviorally-relevant features are the targets of interest in human neuroscience
joint reconstruction-plus-classification supervision constrains latent space toward task structure
small-sample neuroimaging data is prone to overfitting in standard deep models
manipulations: model class (TRACE vs autoencoder vs VAE vs PCA)
two severely truncated benchmark ML datasets
fMRI dataset of 59 subjects viewing animals vs objects
key_findings: TRACE outperformed all compared models nearly unilaterally
up to 12% increase in classification accuracy
up to 56% improvement in recovering cleaner task-relevant representations
interpretations: tying representation learning to behavioral targets yields cleaner, separable neural representations under small-sample regimes
